steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb of the first clause?

hints.inventus: 'Inventus' is a perfect passive participle — it needs 'est' to form a verb phrase
hints.est: 'Est' is the auxiliary for 'inventus' in the second clause

steps.reveal: Perierat = he had perished / he had been lost

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'periit' — 'Perierat' has the -erat pluperfect ending
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'peribat' — 'Perierat' is built on the perfect stem + erat

hints.pario, parere, peperi, partum: These are the parts of 'parere' (to give birth)
hints.perfero, perferre, pertuli, perlatum: These are the parts of 'perferre' (to carry through/endure)
hints.perago, peragere, peregi, peractum: These are the parts of 'peragere' (to accomplish)

steps.reveal: inventus = having been found (perf. pass. ptcp.)

hints.present active participle: A present active participle would be 'inveniens' — 'inventus' ends in -tus
hints.future active participle: A future active participle would be 'inventurus' — 'inventus' is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: A gerundive would be 'inveniendus' — 'inventus' is a completed passive action

steps.reveal: inventus est = he has been found

hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'inventus erat' — 'est' makes this perfect
hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'invenitur' — 'inventus est' is perfect passive
hints.future passive indicative: Future passive would be 'invenietur' — 'inventus est' is perfect

hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the parts of 'venire' (to come) — 'invenire' is a compound: in + venire
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.invito, invitare, invitavi, invitatum: These are the parts of 'invitare' (to invite)

steps.prompt: What is the parallel structure between the two clauses?
steps.target: both contrast being lost with being found (past loss → present recovery)
choices: both contrast being lost with being found (past loss → present recovery)
both clauses are in the same tense

hints.both clauses are in the same tense: 'Perierat' is pluperfect and 'inventus est' is perfect — different tenses showing sequence
hints.both verbs are passive: 'Perierat' is active (intransitive) and 'inventus est' is passive — they differ in voice
hints.both clauses use the subjunctive: Both verbs are indicative — 'perierat' and 'inventus est' state facts

translation: He had been lost and has been found
